157: # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
158: # BUG-B-101 fix: unified manifest.status taxonomy.
159: #
160: # Pre-fix, successful runs wrote manifest.json WITHOUT a "status" key
161: # while abort runs DID include it. Documentation claimed
162: # manifest.status was authoritative — it wasn't. See
163: # outputs/codex_findings/deep_dive_round_1/findings.md for the full
164: # scoping analysis.
165: #
166: # Post-fix, EVERY exit path writes a manifest.json with a "status"
167: # field from this unified taxonomy. `summary["status"]` is preserved
168: # as secondary sweep telemetry for backward compatibility with any
169: # downstream counter that relied on the legacy labels.
170: # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
171: 
172: UNIFIED_STATUS_VALUES: frozenset[str] = frozenset({
173:     # success
174:     "success",
175:     # partial — report produced but degraded signal
176:     "partial_thin_corpus",
177:     "partial_incomplete_corpus",
178:     "partial_rule_check_warnings",
179:     "partial_outline_fallback",      # BUG-M-203: planner failed, fallback used
180:     "partial_evaluator_advisory",    # BUG-M-205: judge flagged critical axes
181:     "partial_qwen_advisory",         # I-modref-004 (#530): legacy alias, historical manifests
182:     # abort — pipeline refused to produce a report
183:     "abort_scope_rejected",
184:     "abort_no_sources",
185:     "abort_corpus_inadequate",
186:     "abort_corpus_approval_denied",
187:     "abort_no_verified_sections",
188:     "abort_evaluator_critical",      # BUG-M-205: PT08/PT11/PT12 integrity failure
189:     # error — unhandled exception
190:     "error_unexpected",
191: })
192: 
193: # Map legacy summary["status"] labels → unified manifest.status values.
194: _SUMMARY_TO_UNIFIED: dict[str, str] = {
195:     "ok": "success",
196:     "ok_thin_corpus": "partial_thin_corpus",
197:     "ok_incomplete_corpus": "partial_incomplete_corpus",
198:     "ok_outline_fallback": "partial_outline_fallback",
199:     "ok_evaluator_advisory": "partial_evaluator_advisory",
200:     "ok_qwen_advisory": "partial_qwen_advisory",  # I-modref-004 (#530): legacy alias
201:     "warn_rule_checks": "partial_rule_check_warnings",
202:     "fail_no_sources": "abort_no_sources",
203:     "fail_no_verified_prose": "abort_no_verified_sections",
204:     "abort_scope_rejected": "abort_scope_rejected",
205:     "abort_corpus_inadequate": "abort_corpus_inadequate",
206:     "abort_corpus_approval_denied": "abort_corpus_approval_denied",
207:     "abort_no_verified_sections": "abort_no_verified_sections",
208:     "abort_evaluator_critical": "abort_evaluator_critical",
209:     # I-meta-002 sub-PR-6: 4-role D8 release decision (single binding gate). Released =>
210:     # success; held => a release-blocking abort (D8 held: fabricated occurrence / coverage
211:     # shortfall / S0 must-cover missing / pending rewrite). Only set on the guarded 4-role path.
212:     "four_role_released": "success",
213:     "four_role_held": "abort_four_role_release_held",
214:     "error": "error_unexpected",
215: }
216: 
217: 
218: def to_unified_status(summary_status: str) -> str:
219:     """Map a legacy summary["status"] label to the unified
220:     manifest.status taxonomy. Unknown labels become error_unexpected
221:     (fail loudly for the reader; still a valid taxonomy value)."""
222:     return _SUMMARY_TO_UNIFIED.get(summary_status, "error_unexpected")
223: 
224: 
225: def expected_str_for_abort(protocol: dict) -> str:
226:     """Render expected tier distribution for abort-artifact text."""
227:     parts = []
228:     for entry in protocol.get("expected_tier_distribution", []) or []:
229:         tier = entry.get("tier")
230:         mn = (entry.get("min_fraction", 0) or 0) * 100
231:         mx = (entry.get("max_fraction", 1) or 1) * 100
232:         if tier:
233:             parts.append(f"{tier} {mn:.0f}-{mx:.0f}%")
234:     return ", ".join(parts) or "per scope template"
235: 
236: 
237: def _retrieval_manifest_section(retrieval) -> dict:
238:     """#958 (S2): the SINGLE source of truth for a manifest's "retrieval"
239:     section. Used by BOTH `_base_manifest_envelope` (abort paths) AND the
240:     inline success-path manifest, so the corpus-truncation flag + counts can
241:     never be omitted on one path. All getattr with defaults (backward
242:     compatible with pre-#958 retrieval objects)."""
243:     return {
244:         "pre_filter": getattr(retrieval, "total_candidates_pre_filter", 0),
245:         "fetched": getattr(retrieval, "candidates_fetched", 0),
246:         "failed": getattr(retrieval, "candidates_failed_fetch", 0),
247:         "api_calls": getattr(retrieval, "api_calls", {}),
248:         # #958: fail-loud corpus-truncation signal (was log-only).

250:         "candidates_total": getattr(retrieval, "candidates_total", 0),
251:         "candidates_processed": getattr(retrieval, "candidates_processed", 0),
252:     }

283: def write_per_run_cost_ledger(run_dir: Path, run_id: str) -> int:
284:     """BUG-M-206 fix (deep-dive R8): filter the global cost ledger for
285:     entries tagged with this run_id and write a per-run copy to
286:     <run_dir>/cost_ledger.jsonl. Returns the count of entries written.
287: 
288:     The global ledger stays authoritative (monotonic append-only log),
289:     but per-run consumers don't have to grep it by run_id anymore.
290:     """
291:     global_path = Path(
292:         os.environ.get("PG_COST_LEDGER_PATH", "logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl")
293:     )
294:     if not global_path.exists():
295:         return 0
296:     out_path = run_dir / "cost_ledger.jsonl"
297:     n = 0
298:     try:
299:         with open(global_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as src, \
300:                 open(out_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as dst:
301:             for line in src:
302:                 line = line.rstrip("\n")
303:                 if not line:
304:                     continue
305:                 try:
306:                     entry = json.loads(line)
307:                 except json.JSONDecodeError:
308:                     continue
309:                 if entry.get("session_id") == run_id:
310:                     dst.write(line + "\n")

24:     """Codex pass-2 reproducer: two async tasks setting different
25:     run_ids should NOT stomp each other. With ContextVar, each task
26:     has its own context."""
27:     from src.polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client import (
28:         set_current_run_id,
29:         current_run_id,
30:     )
31: 
32:     async def task_a() -> tuple[str, str | None]:
33:         set_current_run_id("run_A")
34:         await asyncio.sleep(0.05)
35:         # After task B has had time to set its own run_id, A's should
36:         # still be run_A.
37:         return ("A", current_run_id())
38: 
39:     async def task_b() -> tuple[str, str | None]:
40:         await asyncio.sleep(0.01)

42:         await asyncio.sleep(0.08)
43:         return ("B", current_run_id())
44: 
45:     async def main() -> list:
46:         return await asyncio.gather(task_a(), task_b())
47: 
48:     results = asyncio.run(main())
49:     # Convert to dict for readability
50:     rids = {label: rid for label, rid in results}
51:     assert rids["A"] == "run_A", (
52:         f"Task A's run_id was stomped: got {rids['A']}, expected run_A"
53:     )
54:     assert rids["B"] == "run_B"

15: def test_b4_deepseek_tokens_impute_nonzero() -> None:
16:     mod = _mod()
17:     cost = mod._impute_cost_from_tokens(
18:         "deepseek/deepseek-v3.2-exp", 5000, 500, 0,
19:     )
20:     # DeepSeek rates: $0.27 in / $0.38 out per M

51: def test_b4_budget_guard_not_bypassable_when_cost_missing(
52:     monkeypatch,
53: ) -> None:
54:     """If OpenRouter omits cost but tokens were used, the budget guard
55:     must still accumulate a non-zero amount per call."""
56:     monkeypatch.setenv("PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN", "0.10")
57:     import src.polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client as mod
58:     importlib.reload(mod)
59:     mod.reset_run_cost()
60:     # Simulate what the _call() code path does when api_cost is None
61:     # and tokens were consumed: the imputed cost is fed into _add_run_cost.
62:     for _ in range(10):
63:         imputed = mod._impute_cost_from_tokens(
64:             "unknown-vendor/mystery", 2000, 500, 0,
65:         )
66:         assert imputed > 0
67:         mod._add_run_cost(imputed)
68:     # 10 calls × ~$0.0135 imputed > $0.10 budget → check_run_budget raises
69:     # (10 × (2000*3 + 500*15)/1M = 0.135)
70:     assert mod.current_run_cost() > 0.10
71:     import pytest
72:     with pytest.raises(mod.BudgetExceededError):
73:         mod.check_run_budget()
74:     monkeypatch.setenv("PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN", "0.10")
75:     importlib.reload(mod)

78: def test_b4_negative_tokens_clamped_to_zero() -> None:
79:     """Codex round 5 probe: a corrupted API response with negative
80:     token counts must NOT produce a negative cost that would silently
81:     reduce the accumulated run budget."""
82:     mod = _mod()
83:     # All-negative: returns 0
84:     assert mod._impute_cost_from_tokens("deepseek/x", -100, -50, -10) == 0.0
85:     # Partial negative: negative inputs clamp to 0, positives still count
86:     cost = mod._impute_cost_from_tokens(
87:         "deepseek/deepseek-v3.2-exp", -100, 50, 0,
88:     )
89:     assert cost >= 0.0, f"Cost must be non-negative, got {cost}"
90:     # Should equal the cost of (0 input, 50 output, 0 reasoning)
91:     expected = 50 * 0.38 / 1_000_000
92:     assert abs(cost - expected) < 1e-9

3820:     async def _m63_llm_call(prompt: str) -> tuple[str, int, int]:
3821:         """Adapter: one OpenRouter call per M-58 slot prompt.
3822:         Returns (response_text, input_tokens, output_tokens).
3823:         M-58's `parse_slot_fill_response` handles the JSON
3824:         parsing; we just hand the raw text through.
3825: 
3826:         V30 Phase-2 M-66 run-5 diagnostic: contract slots with
3827:         25K-char direct_quote (e.g. FDA Mounjaro label via
3828:         M-66b-T OA full-text fetch) produced JSON truncation
3829:         (`Unterminated string starting at pos 10561`) when the
3830:         LLM tried to echo a long regulatory prose span under the
3831:         default section_max_tokens=2400 budget. Raise the cap
3832:         for contract extraction calls — the JSON schema is much
3833:         terser than legacy section prose (max 10 fields × verbatim
3834:         quotes × ~500 chars = ~5K tokens), so 6000 gives safe
3835:         headroom without inviting runaway verbosity.
3836:         """
3837:         from ..llm.openrouter_client import (
3838:             OpenRouterClient,
3839:             set_reasoning_call_context,
3840:         )
3841:         client = OpenRouterClient(model=gen_model)
3842:         try:
3843:             # I-gen-004 (#496): tag the V30 contract-slot extraction call.
3844:             set_reasoning_call_context(
3845:                 section="_contract_slot", call_type="contract_slot",
3846:             )
3847:             response = await client.generate(
3848:                 prompt=prompt,
3849:                 system=(
3850:                     "You are a JSON-only extraction assistant. "
3851:                     "Output ONLY the JSON schema the user prompt "
3852:                     "specifies. Do not include prose, preamble, "
3853:                     "code fences, or any text outside the JSON "
3854:                     "object."
3855:                 ),
3856:                 max_tokens=max(section_max_tokens, 6000),
3857:                 temperature=section_temperature,
3858:             )
3859:         finally:
3860:             if hasattr(client, "close"):
3861:                 try:
3862:                     await client.close()
3863:                 except Exception:
3864:                     pass
3865:         return (
3866:             (response.content or "").strip(),
3867:             response.input_tokens,
3868:             response.output_tokens,
3869:         )
3870: 
3871:     # V33 (M-72) cross-trial synthesis: contract sections must
3872:     # render BEFORE legacy sections so the synthesis block has
3873:     # access to extracted slot payloads. Pre-V33 ordering ran
3874:     # everything concurrently; post-V33, contract runs first,
3875:     # then legacy runs with the synthesis block.
3876:     contract_plans = [p for p in plans if is_contract_section(p)]
3877:     legacy_plans = [p for p in plans if not is_contract_section(p)]
3878: 
3879:     async def _run_contract_bounded(plan: SectionPlan) -> SectionResult:
3880:         async with sem:
3881:             result, payloads = await run_contract_section(
3882:                 plan, evidence_pool,
3883:                 llm_call=_m63_llm_call,
3884:                 section_result_cls=SectionResult,
3885:                 strict_verify_fn=strict_verify,
3886:                 rewrite_fn=_rewrite_draft_with_spans,
3887:             )
3888:             contract_slot_payloads.extend(payloads)
3889:             return result
3890: 
3891:     contract_results = await asyncio.gather(*[
3892:         _run_contract_bounded(p) for p in contract_plans
3893:     ])
3894: 
3895:     # V33 M-72: build the cross-trial synthesis block AFTER contract
3896:     # payloads land. Empty block when fewer than 2 trial frames
3897:     # have extracted content.
3898:     from .cross_trial_synthesis import build_cross_trial_synthesis
3899:     cross_trial_block = build_cross_trial_synthesis(
3900:         contract_slot_payloads,
3901:     )
3902: 
3903:     async def _run_legacy_bounded(plan: SectionPlan) -> SectionResult:
3904:         async with sem:
3905:             return await _run_section(
3906:                 plan, evidence_pool,
3907:                 model=gen_model,
3908:                 temperature=section_temperature,
3909:                 max_tokens_per_section=section_max_tokens,
3910:                 min_kept_fraction=min_kept_fraction,
3911:                 contradictions=contradictions,
3912:                 cross_trial_block=cross_trial_block,
3913:             )

outputs\audits\continuous\4f20140_audit.md:30:- **C-sec-f28-test-honesty (probe 7):** PASS-with-note. The new sweep at `accessibility.spec.ts:156-194` throws a custom `Error` with rich diagnostics rather than using `expect(small).toEqual([])`. Playwright's reporter treats uncaught Errors in `test()` callbacks as test failures (this is standard behavior — `test.fail` semantics), so CI reporting is intact. The diagnostic message lists offending elements with size/text — strictly better than `expect()`'s default. No security risk; out-of-lens P3 observation only.
outputs\audits\continuous\4f20140_audit.md:50:**P3.1 — F-26 default selection + first-Tab UX.** Native `radiogroup` ARIA pattern usually pairs with arrow-key cycling (Up/Down/Left/Right move between radios) rather than Tab cycling. The F-28 keyboard test (`accessibility.spec.ts:244-263`) uses `radios.nth(1).focus()` directly + Space; doesn't validate arrow-key behavior. WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices says radiogroup MUST handle arrow keys to cycle (and Tab moves OUT of the group, not within). With each `<button role="radio">` keeping its own `tabindex=0` (the default for `<button>`), Tab moves through every radio one-by-one — which works but isn't the ARIA-conformant pattern. Not a security issue (the form behavior is correct), and not strictly a WCAG-AA failure. **Tag: out-of-scope-observed (a11y polish for cycle-12).**

outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_1\findings.md:20:  Pipeline A is materially more hardened than the pre-audit baseline, but this scoping pass found three cross-cutting blocker-class issues that are design/control-plane failures, not just isolated bugs. The most serious are that scope gating never actually aborts, the active UI path bypasses the hardened invariants entirely, and the success-manifest contract is internally inconsistent enough that downstream consumers cannot rely on `manifest.json` as documented. The next tier of risk is silent under-coverage: generation accepts collapsed outlines, contradictions detection is narrow, limitations bypass provenance, and evaluator outputs are advisory rather than enforcing.

outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_5\findings.md:9:  The evaluator currently produces useful signals but no authoritative release decision. Rule checks are partly deterministic and partly keyword/shape heuristics; the Qwen judge is non-same-family but sees only the report text, not the evidence pool. Therefore a single Qwen `needs_revision` should not always abort a run, but critical evaluator failures must prevent `success`. The fix should add an explicit evaluator gate that distinguishes release-blocking deterministic integrity failures, critical Qwen verdict patterns, and advisory quality warnings.
outputs\codex_findings\deep_dive_round_5\findings.md:26:The orchestrator writes evaluator artifacts and logs Qwen verdicts, but status selection ignores Qwen entirely. outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_10\codex_stderr.log:229:outputs/full_scale_v21/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm/report.md:9:Gastrointestinal adverse events were the most commonly reported adverse events with tirzepatide across phase 3 trials in type 2 diabetes.[1][9] In the SURPASS-AP-Combo trial in an Asia-Pacific population, gastrointestinal adverse event incidence was generally similar across subgroups defined by age, sex, baseline HbA1c, BMI, body weight, diabetes duration, and concomitant oral medications.[7] A systematic review and meta-analysis of nine RCTs concluded the gastrointestinal adverse event profile of tirzepatide was consistent with GLP-1 receptor agonists, with incidences of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea higher than placebo but similar to active comparators like semaglutide and dulaglutide.[10] A systematic review and meta-analysis concluded tirzepatide did not increase events of blood glucose <54 mg/dL or <70 mg/dL compared to control overall.[11] However, when tirzepatide is added to insulin or sulfonylureas, the risk increases; in SURPASS-AP-Combo, where patients were on metformin with or without a sulfonylurea, the incidence of hypoglycemia (blood glucose ≤70 mg/dL or severe) was higher in patients using a concomitant sulfonylurea (e.g., 43.1% with tirzepatide 5 mg) compared to those on metformin alone (6.6% with tirzepatide 5 mg).[7] One meta-analysis specifically noted tirzepatide 15 mg was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83), while another network meta-analysis found tirzepatide showed a higher severe hypoglycemia risk compared to semaglutide and liraglutide.[10][12] In clinical trials, no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) were reported; for instance, the SURMOUNT-4 trial specifically stated no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma or pancreatic cancer were reported.[13] However, when compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists, tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer.[14] A 2026 evidence review summarizing multiple large database studies concluded there is no evidence of increased thyroid cancer risk with GLP-1 receptor agonist use, including tirzepatide, over 1.8-3.0 years of follow-up.[15] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal cardiovascular and obesity outcome trials, including the SURPASS program, did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[16]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\codex_stderr.log:310:The most frequent adverse events associated with tirzepatide are gastrointestinal in nature, with nausea, diarrhea, and vomiting being the most common and typically mild to moderate in severity.[5] In the SURPASS Phase 3 clinical trials for type 2 diabetes, the incidence of gastrointestinal adverse events was dose-dependent, with nausea reported in 20–33% of participants, diarrhea in 18–22%, and vomiting in 8–13%.[5] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials in patients with diabetes and/or obesity similarly reports that gastrointestinal effects are the most common adverse events.[6] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, the overall incidence of side effects was 26%, with nausea being the most common at 15%, and only 4% of patients discontinued tirzepatide due to side effects.[7] In the SURMOUNT-1 trial for obesity, the vast majority of participants (90%) who were initially late responders (achieving <5% weight loss at week 12) went on to achieve clinically meaningful weight reduction by week 72, suggesting a generally tolerable profile that allows for continued treatment.[8] Serious adverse events appear uncommon; in a Phase I trial of a bispecific GLP-1/GLP-2 agonist, no serious adverse events or discontinuations due to adverse events occurred, and treatment-emergent adverse events were mostly mild to moderate gastrointestinal events.[9] Furthermore, in the retrospective study of patients with type 1 diabetes, no episodes of severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis were recorded during tirzepatide treatment.[7]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:322:In older patients with type 2 diabetes and diminished β-cell function, a case report suggests tirzepatide may help control postprandial hyperglycemia but may not replace basal insulin for fasting control, requiring monitoring for sarcopenia.[21] In a prespecified analysis of the SUMMIT trial in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide's effect on cardiovascular outcomes was consistent regardless of diabetes status, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 (95% CI: 0.35-1.15) for the composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure in patients with type 2 diabetes and 0.61 (95% CI: 0.33-1.10) in those without diabetes (Pinteraction = 0.95).[22] However, weight loss with tirzepatide was less pronounced in patients with type 2 diabetes (10.4% [95% CI: 8.7%-12.2%]) compared to those without diabetes (12.9% [95% CI: 11.2%-14.6%]) (Pinteraction = 0.04).[22] Weight loss was significantly greater in patients with higher baseline body weight (≥75 kg) across all tirzepatide doses.[23] A separate post-hoc analysis of SURPASS trials indicated that tirzepatide was effective in reducing HbA1c and body weight in patients with glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibodies (GADA-positive), though the HbA1c reduction was slightly less compared to GADA-negative patients (estimated treatment difference 0.21% [95% CI: 0.03, 0.39]; p=0.024).[24]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:708:Tirzepatide demonstrated significant, dose-dependent improvements in both glycemic control and body weight across the SURPASS clinical trials in adults with type 2 diabetes.[1] The A1C reduction from baseline to the primary end point ranged from −1.87% with tirzepatide 5 mg in SURPASS-1 to −2.59% with tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg in SURPASS-5.[1] In SURPASS-1, the highest dose (15 mg) reduced A1C by 2.07% from baseline and 52% of participants achieved normoglycemia (A1c <5.7%).[1][2] The proportion of participants achieving an A1C value <7.0% with tirzepatide across the trials ranged from 81 to 97%.[1] For weight loss, body weight reductions from baseline at the primary end point ranged from −6.2 kg (−6.6%) with tirzepatide 5 mg when added to insulin glargine (SURPASS-5) to −12.9 kg (−13.9%) with tirzepatide 15 mg when added to metformin with or without an SGLT2 inhibitor (SURPASS-3).[1] Tirzepatide was superior to active comparators, including semaglutide 1 mg and various insulin regimens, in reducing A1C and body weight.[1][3] These effects were consistent across different patient populations and background therapies.[1][4]
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_4\_stale_v7_misdispatch\codex_stderr.log:724:In a post-hoc analysis of SURPASS-1 to -5, tirzepatide demonstrated significantly greater reductions in HbA1c versus comparators in both prespecified age subgroups (<65 and ≥65 years).[23] Regarding sex, all tirzepatide doses significantly reduced body weight compared to comparators regardless of the sex of study participants across SURPASS-1 to -5.[24] In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity, tirzepatide produced less weight loss at 52 weeks in those with type 2 diabetes (10.4%) than in those without diabetes (12.9%).[25] However, the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar between patients with or without diabetes, with a hazard ratio of 0.64 for patients with diabetes and 0.61 for those without.[25] In a retrospective study of adults with type 1 diabetes and obesity, tirzepatide led to significant weight loss, with a median total body weight loss of 8% at 6 months and a 1% reduction in HbA1c.[26] For individuals with type 2 diabetes who were glutamic acid decarboxylase autoantibody-positive, tirzepatide was effective, achieving HbA1c reductions of -2.11% and body weight reductions of -9.2 kg at 40/42 weeks, though HbA1c reductions were slightly greater in GADA-negative patients.[27] The most frequent adverse events across subgroups were mild-to-moderate and gastrointestinal-related, occurring during dose-escalation.[23][24]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_8\codex_stderr.log:468:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most frequently reported side effects of tirzepatide, with nausea, diarrhea, constipation, and vomiting being common.[4][9] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials noted the overall safety profile of tirzepatide is similar to GLP-1 receptor agonists, with gastrointestinal events being a primary characteristic.[9][10] In the SURMOUNT-4 trial involving participants with obesity but without diabetes, gastrointestinal events such as nausea (35.5%), diarrhea (21.1%), constipation (20.7%), and vomiting (16.3%) were common during the lead-in period, and most were mild to moderate.[5][6] During the double-blind period of that trial, gastrointestinal events remained more common with continued tirzepatide than with placebo (e.g., diarrhea 10.7% vs 4.8%; nausea 8.1% vs 2.7%).[6] Real-world pharmacovigilance analyses confirm disproportionate reporting of gastrointestinal disorders, including nausea (reporting odds ratio [ROR] 4.01, 95% CI 3.85–4.19) and diarrhea.[11][10] However, the same systematic review indicated the 15 mg dose was associated with more hypoglycemia than GLP-1 receptor agonists (pooled RR=3.83, 95% CI 1.19–12.30).[9] Regarding thyroid safety, while clinical trials like SURMOUNT-4 reported no cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma, real-world disproportionality analyses have detected a signal for medullary thyroid cancer (ROR 13.67, 95% CI 4.35–42.96) compared to all other drugs.[5][6][10] Another analysis also reported medullary thyroid carcinoma signals.[11] A case report has described painless biphasic thyroiditis following tirzepatide use, though thyroid function normalized after discontinuation.[12] For other safety signals, real-world analyses indicate disproportionate reporting of pancreatitis (ROR 3.63, 95% CI 3.15–4.19) and diabetic retinopathy (ROR 4.14, 95% CI 2.34–7.30).[11][10] However, in the SURMOUNT-4 trial, there were no adjudication-confirmed cases of pancreatitis.[6] Discontinuation rates due to adverse events may be dose-dependent; one meta-analysis found tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with significantly higher risks of discontinuation due to adverse events compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[9]
outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_8\codex_stderr.log:480:A post-hoc analysis across the five global SURPASS trials reported that the efficacy of tirzepatide on HbA1c reduction was consistent in prespecified age subgroups (<65 years and ≥65 years) compared to placebo or active comparators.[20] For patients with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk, the SURPASS-4 trial specifically enrolled this population, comparing tirzepatide to insulin glargine.[21] Furthermore, a prespecified analysis of the SUMMIT trial in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and obesity found that the effect of tirzepatide on the primary composite endpoint of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure was similar in participants with type 2 diabetes (HR 0.64) and without diabetes (HR 0.61), with no significant interaction.[22] Regarding dedicated cardiovascular outcomes, the SURPASS-CVOT trial compared tirzepatide to dulaglutide in participants with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, with baseline characteristics showing a mean age of 64.1 years, diabetes duration of 14.7 years, HbA1c of 8.4%, and BMI of 32.6 kg/m².[23] Results from SURPASS-CVOT indicated tirzepatide was noninferior to dulaglutide for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE-3), with a hazard ratio of 0.92, and was associated with an 8% lower risk of MACE-3 and a 16% lower risk of all-cause mortality compared to dulaglutide.[24]

outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_6\codex_stderr.log:536:Gastrointestinal adverse events are the most common tolerability concern with tirzepatide and are typically mild to moderate in severity.[6] A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials reported that gastrointestinal adverse events were the most frequently reported category in tirzepatide-related individual case safety reports in a real-world pharmacovigilance database, with pancreatitis and vomiting being the most common specific events.[7] For vomiting, incidence with tirzepatide 15 mg ranged from 6% to 18.1%, and for diarrhea, it ranged from 12% to 31%.[1] One systematic review found that compared to 5 mg, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg were associated with more frequent nausea, and 15 mg led to more diarrhea than 5 mg, suggesting some dose-dependence for specific gastrointestinal events.[8] The risk of hypoglycemia with tirzepatide is generally low when not used with insulin or insulin secretagogues.[9] Regarding thyroid safety, real-world pharmacovigilance data has detected a disproportionate reporting signal for medullary thyroid cancer with tirzepatide (ROR 13.67, 95% CI 4.35–42.96).[10] This aligns with a boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors seen in rodents, and it is unknown whether tirzepatide causes such tumors in humans, including medullary thyroid carcinoma.[11] However, the same real-world analysis noted tirzepatide exhibited a similar risk of medullary thyroid cancer compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[10] Furthermore, a review noted that pivotal trials in the SURPASS program did not report clinically meaningful increases in autoimmune thyroid disease or sustained thyroid dysfunction.[12] Discontinuation due to adverse events appears dose-related; one systematic review found the risk of discontinuation by adverse event was significantly higher with tirzepatide 10 mg (pooled RR=1.75, 95%CI[1.16-2.63]) and 15 mg (pooled RR=2.03, 95%CI [1.37-3.01]) compared to GLP-1 receptor agonists.[8]

outputs\codex_findings\full_scale_pass_2\findings.md:8:  M-16's conference-ID and narrative-flavor title detectors are narrow, order-safe, and covered by focused regressions; they do not appear to add material false-positive risk when the discriminating phrase is actually present in the resolved title. The remaining three hallucinations are not primarily classifier-rule failures, but evidence-signal availability failures: the live pipeline sends truncated or non-diagnostic titles into a title-only classifier. PubMed E-utilities would likely repair the two PMC title-recovery misses, but it would not address the Oxford case whose title does not identify the article as a meta-analysis. A bounded body-inspection pass over high-signal regions such as fetched title/H1/abstract/article-type/early page text is the smallest fix that can cover all three without spending a v3 sweep on a known residual failure mode.

outputs\codex_findings\v30_sweep_integration_audit\findings.md:6:1. `PG_V30_ENABLED=0` is functionally safe once `run_v30_post_generation()` is reached: it returns immediately with `enabled=False` before the inner chain does any V30 work, and the runner only mutates the manifest inside `if v30_result.enabled` (`src/polaris_graph/v30_sweep_integration.py:117`, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1661`). Caveat: the runner still imports and calls V30 unconditionally, so an import-time failure would still stamp `manifest["v30_error"]` even when disabled (`scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1650`, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1701`). Good enough for this commit, but not perfectly hermetic gating.
outputs\codex_findings\v30_sweep_integration_audit\findings.md:7:2. Exception containment is strong enough to keep the sweep alive. Inner-chain failures are converted into `V30SweepResult.error` (`src/polaris_graph/v30_sweep_integration.py:127`), and the runner has a second broad guard around the integration block (`scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1650`, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1701`). I do not see a path where V30 overwrites existing manifest keys other than adding `v30_*` and optional `frame_coverage_report`; the bigger issue is semantic overclaiming, not structural corruption.
outputs\codex_findings\v30_sweep_integration_audit\findings.md:12:7. Using the contract DOI is the right choice. If M-56 retrieval fails and the row DOI is `None`, validating against the row DOI would reject the exact human completion that is supposed to repair the gap; the contract DOI is the stable identity anchor (`src/polaris_graph/v30_sweep_integration.py:357`). The acceptance/rejection tests are aligned with that contract-DOI behavior (`tests/polaris_graph/test_v30_sweep_integration.py:351`, `tests/polaris_graph/test_v30_sweep_integration.py:416`).
outputs\codex_findings\v30_sweep_integration_audit\findings.md:21:- **Medium** `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1650`, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1701` — Opt-in gating is not fully hermetic because the runner imports/calls V30 even when disabled. outputs\codex_findings\m58_code_audit\findings.md:21:- Blocker: `parse_slot_fill_response` accepts fabricated extracted values as long as the model supplies any real substring for `source_span`. The parser checks `source_span in direct_quote` but never checks that `value` is supported by that span, so `value="1880"` with `source_span="N=1879"` passes and later renders false prose. Add a hard check that extracted `value` is a verbatim substring of `source_span` (or `direct_quote` under a defined normalization) and add a failure test for that case. `src/polaris_graph/generator/slot_fill.py:315` `tests/polaris_graph/test_m58_slot_fill.py:312`
outputs\codex_findings\m58_code_audit\findings.md:22:- Medium: `compose_gap_payload` has no guard that `frame_row.provenance_class` is actually `FRAME_GAP_UNRECOVERABLE`. A misrouted non-gap row would be silently converted into all-`gap_unrecoverable`, which is worse than failing fast because it erases retrievable evidence. Mirror the prompt-builder guard here. `src/polaris_graph/generator/slot_fill.py:366` `tests/polaris_graph/test_m58_slot_fill.py:373`

outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_6\findings.md:31:`ADVISORY_RULES` is symmetric with the existing gate maps: rule ID to stable reason code. The implementation appends `advisory_pt13_unhedged_superlatives` to `reasons`, does not append PT13 to `rule_blockers`, and does not set `abort_on_rule`. As a result, a PT13-only failure leaves `gate_class=pass` and `release_allowed=True`, while other abort/partial causes still dominate. That matches the intended advisory semantics.

outputs\codex_findings\m8_review\findings.md:8:- The larger issue is the paused-side state machine. In the current control flow, `JobControl.Paused` exits `runner.run()`, and the worker returns after `mark_paused()`. That means no live executor remains attached to a `paused` job. Under that model, `paused -> running` is not a real transition; it strands the row. The legitimate Phase B resume edge is `paused -> pending`, not `paused -> running`. `paused -> failed` is also not a real runtime edge today unless you add a distinct `resuming` state or worker heartbeat/ownership.
outputs\codex_findings\m8_review\findings.md:33:- Change resume to `paused -> pending`; add `paused -> pending` to the allowed graph and remove `paused -> running` unless you also add worker ownership/heartbeat. Consider removing `paused -> failed` too under the current model.

outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_4\findings.md:18:The fallback cascade in `_fetch_content()` is mostly correct for the documented policy. outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_4\findings.md:20:The main correctness gap is the unbounded `worker.join()` at `live_retriever.py:304`. AccessBypass has many internal timeouts, including Crawl4AI `wait_for(..., timeout=timeout_seconds + 10)` (`access_bypass.py:673-678`) and aiohttp timeouts, but not every failure mode is covered: browser startup (`__aenter__`), browser cleanup (`__aexit__`), imports, native extension work, or subprocess wedges can still block the worker. Reproducer outline: monkeypatch `src.tools.access_bypass.AccessBypass.fetch_with_bypass` to `async def` await an unset `asyncio.Event()`, call `_fetch_content()`, and it never returns because the caller joins without a timeout.

outputs\codex_findings\m31_code_audit\findings.md:19:The lenient regex is appropriately narrow for this fix. It only runs after strict `json.loads(payload)` fails, so it cannot alter any well-formed JSON fast path. The transformation removes only commas immediately before `]` or `}`. It does not synthesize missing commas between adjacent JSON values or objects, so missing-comma truncation still returns `json_decode_error`; the added truncation test verifies this behavior.

outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:3:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:14:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.
outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:16:On paywalls: yes, this is achievable autonomously up to a ceiling, but not to competitor parity in every clinical dimension. Highest-quality clinical synthesis does require reliable access to primary full text. If the clamp paper or a NEJM/Lancet primary is paywalled and POLARIS cannot legally fetch enough text to verify quantitative claims, then POLARIS cannot honestly match a system whose model already internalized those details or whose product has licensed access. You should stop pretending architecture alone erases that. Architecture can eliminate self-inflicted failure; it cannot conjure inaccessible evidence.
outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_14\findings.md:10:  Cycle 6 is the 8-query run in outputs/sweep_r3_final: two clinical reports released as partial_thin_corpus with release_allowed=true, and the other six runs aborted before synthesis. The DOI exact-lookup path is working for the Frontiers DOI case, which is now T2 via R9_openalex_sr_or_ma. The MDPI no-DOI URL remains T1 with the old truncated title, so the content-title fallback is not reliably reaching the classifier. Six false T1 labels remain, all from broad domain-presumed-primary fallback behavior rather than verified primary-study metadata. The six aborts are legitimate because correcting the observed false T1s would not satisfy their failed corpus thresholds.
outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_14\findings.md:67:All six are low-confidence presumed-primary classifications. The recurring failure class is: when DOI/OpenAlex metadata is absent or insufficient and the visible title does not contain explicit review/guideline markers, journal-like domains and even `acc.org` PDFs can still be promoted to T1 by `R10_journal_domain_presumed_primary`.

outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:7:The ceiling V28 hit is equally real. The four LOSE_BOTH dimensions are not independent failures. Citations, claim frames, structural depth, and narrative depth all failed because the report did not make the pivotal primary publications the spine of the answer. SURPASS-2 was cited through a T4 post-hoc instead of the Frías NEJM primary; SURPASS-4 Del Prato and SURPASS-CVOT Nicholls were in live_corpus but did not reach the final bibliography or prose; mechanism remained review-grade because primary clamp evidence did not become extracted narrative. Once the primary frame was missing, every downstream artifact became compensatory: trial tables were thin, per-trial summaries selected easier trials instead of the important ones, and mechanism prose grew longer without becoming more primary-evidence-dense.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:11:The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:17:Stage 1: build an outline skeleton from pivotal primary publications only. For tirzepatide/T2D, reserve anchors for SURPASS-1, SURPASS-2, SURPASS-3, SURPASS-4, SURPASS-5, SURPASS-6, SURPASS-CVOT, and relevant SURMOUNT trials with population-scope labels. Each anchor needs the primary publication, design, N, population, comparator, endpoint, timepoint, primary effect estimates, uncertainty when available, and safety/caveat field. If a primary is in live_corpus but not selected, selection fails. If a primary is unreachable or quote-thin, refetch runs. If refetch still cannot support numeric extraction, the report says so explicitly instead of silently substituting post-hocs or reviews.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:25:Delta is useful only as a validation sidecar, not as a reason to pause. That is pipeline-wide enough to fix now.

Expected outcome: primary-trial dimensions become stable rather than cycle-variant. Risk: medium-high because it changes pipeline control flow, but it attacks the dominant root cause.

outputs\audits\continuous\61da4ad_audit.md:72:**P1.3 — WCAG 4.1.3 (Status Messages, Level AA) failure: scope-rejection card not announced and focus not moved.** When the user clicks "Check scope" and the backend returns `verdict: "rejected"`, `dashboard/page.tsx:334-367` renders a Card with `border-destructive/60`. Live probe confirms: `aria-live: null`, `role: null`, focus remains on the now-disabled Check-scope button (`focusedTag: "BODY"` after rejection — focus actually escaped to body, an even worse outcome).
outputs\audits\continuous\61da4ad_audit.md:74:For a screen-reader user, the rejection is silent. They press Check scope, hear nothing, and have no signal that they need to reframe. outputs\audits\continuous\61da4ad_audit.md:84:**P2.1 — Inspector tab buttons lack ARIA tab semantics.** The 6 tab buttons at `inspector/[runId]/page.tsx:194-208` render as bare `<button>` elements: `role: null`, `ariaSelected: null`, `ariaControls: null`. Their parent `<nav>` has no `role="tablist"`. WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide recommends `role="tab"` + `aria-selected="true"` + `aria-controls={tabpanel-id}` + parent `role="tablist"` for this pattern. axe doesn't flag because axe's `tablist`/`tab` rules only fire when *partial* ARIA tab semantics are present (e.g., `role="tab"` without `role="tablist"` parent). Bare-button tabs pass axe but degrade screen-reader UX: NVDA/VoiceOver announce each as "Executive summary 3, button" instead of "Executive summary 3, tab, 1 of 6, selected". Not a strict WCAG-AA failure (visible labels + active state are clear; keyboard nav works via Tab); P2 because it's a known-better pattern with code in scope.
outputs\audits\continuous\61da4ad_audit.md:94:**P3.2 — Inspector heading hierarchy is shallow.** Only one `<h1>` (the question). The 6 tab panels rendered into `<main>` use `CardTitle` (likely `<div>` or styled `<p>` per shadcn defaults). A screen-reader user navigating by heading (`H` key in NVDA) skips immediately past the question to next-page. Best-practice; not a strict WCAG-AA failure. Tag: **out-of-scope-observed.**

outputs\audits\continuous\4fe03f7_audit.md:50:**P1.3 — Perf budgets are 4-5x looser than measured baselines.** `DOMContentLoaded < 2000ms` passes when local actual is ~450ms (4.4x slack). `FCP < 1500ms` passes when local actual is ~373ms (4x slack). `Vega < 2500ms` vs ~1.0s actual (2.5x). A 3x performance regression — e.g. a lazy-loaded chunk going eager, blocking 1200ms — would slide through every gate. The author flagged this as their own P0 in the brief; my independent assessment confirms. Recommend `2x baseline` rule: budget = max(local_baseline × 2, hard_floor). For the 250ms tab-switch budget that's ~tight enough; for the others it's not. To verify: run with artificial latency injection (DevTools throttling or `--cpu-throttling-rate=4`) and confirm at least one budget should fail.
outputs\audits\continuous\4fe03f7_audit.md:54:**P2.1 — Tab-switch test masks >1s regressions as locator-timeout, not budget-failure.** `performance.spec.ts:43` and `:62` use `waitFor({timeout: 1_000})` then `expect(switchMs).toBeLessThan(250)`. If the tab takes 1100ms to render, the locator throws TimeoutError before the budget assertion runs — the failure surfaces as flaky-locator, not a perf regression. Fix: bump `waitFor` timeout to e.g. 5000ms so the perf assertion always runs and produces a clean diagnostic. Verify: introduce `await page.waitForTimeout(1100)` after `tab.click()` and confirm the failure mode reads `expected switchMs < 250, got 1100+ms` rather than `locator timed out`.
outputs\audits\continuous\4fe03f7_audit.md:58:**P2.3 — `visual.spec.ts` exclusion is comment-only.** The CI doesn't run `visual.spec.ts` because the workflow invokes per-file (`tests/e2e/inspector.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/accessibility.spec.ts`, `tests/e2e/performance.spec.ts`). But the rationale is comment-only at line 132-135. If anyone refactors the e2e step into `npx playwright test` (no file arg), visual.spec.ts WILL run on Linux, find no matching `*-chromium-linux.png` baseline, and either auto-snapshot (per Playwright defaults the first run creates the baseline — turning a missing baseline into a silent commit-needed) or hard-fail. Add `testIgnore` to `playwright.config.ts` for the file under Linux, OR add `--ignore-snapshots` flag on Linux, OR rename file to skip-by-pattern. Verify: actually run `npx playwright test --project=chromium tests/e2e/visual.spec.ts` on Linux and observe whether it errors loud or silently writes new baseline files.
outputs\audits\continuous\4fe03f7_audit.md:60:**P2.4 — `package-lock.json` cache key uses `web/package-lock.json` path but the e2e job's `npm ci` runs from `web/` (via `defaults.run.working-directory`).** This is correct in practice. outputs\audits\continuous\4f20140_audit.md:30:- **C-sec-f28-test-honesty (probe 7):** PASS-with-note. outputs\audits\continuous\4f20140_audit.md:50:**P3.1 — F-26 default selection + first-Tab UX.** Native `radiogroup` ARIA pattern usually pairs with arrow-key cycling (Up/Down/Left/Right move between radios) rather than Tab cycling. The F-28 keyboard test (`accessibility.spec.ts:244-263`) uses `radios.nth(1).focus()` directly + Space; doesn't validate arrow-key behavior. WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices says radiogroup MUST handle arrow keys to cycle (and Tab moves OUT of the group, not within). With each `<button role="radio">` keeping its own `tabindex=0` (the default for `<button>`), Tab moves through every radio one-by-one — which works but isn't the ARIA-conformant pattern. Not a security issue (the form behavior is correct), and not strictly a WCAG-AA failure. **Tag: out-of-scope-observed (a11y polish for cycle-12).**

 succeeded in 1786ms:
390: 
391: # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
392: # Dispatcher
393: # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
394: 
395: 
396: @dataclass
397: class DomainBackendResult:
398:     domain: str
399:     candidates: list[SearchCandidate]
400:     backends_used: list[str]
401:     per_backend_counts: dict[str, int]
402: 
403: 
404: def run_domain_backends(
405:     *,
406:     domain: str,
407:     research_question: str,
408:     amplified_queries: list[str] | None = None,
409:     max_hits_per_backend: int = PG_DOMAIN_MAX_HITS,
410: ) -> DomainBackendResult:
411:     """Run all applicable domain backends for the given domain.
412: 
413:     For each domain, returns a merged list of SearchCandidate that the
414:     caller can concatenate with the generic Serper+S2 retrieval.
415:     Fail-open: backends that raise return empty lists.
416:     """
417:     queries: list[str] = [research_question]
418:     if amplified_queries:
419:         queries.extend(amplified_queries[:3])   # cap amplified count
420: 
421:     candidates: list[SearchCandidate] = []
422:     used: list[str] = []
423:     per: dict[str, int] = {}
424: 
425:     def _run(name: str, fn) -> None:
426:         nonlocal candidates, used, per
427:         try:
428:             got: list[SearchCandidate] = []
429:             for q in queries:
430:                 got.extend(fn(q, limit=max_hits_per_backend))
431:                 if len(got) >= max_hits_per_backend * 2:
432:                     break
433:             # Dedup by URL
434:             seen_urls = {c.url for c in candidates}
435:             new = [c for c in got if c.url and c.url not in seen_urls]
436:             candidates.extend(new)
437:             used.append(name)
438:             per[name] = len(new)
439:         except Exception as exc:
440:             logger.warning("[domain_backends] %s failed: %s", name, exc)
441:             per[name] = 0
442: 
443:     if domain == "tech":
444:         _run("arxiv", arxiv_search)
445:         _run("github", github_search_repos)
446:     elif domain == "policy":
447:         _run("serper_policy", policy_targeted_serper)
448:     elif domain == "due_diligence":
449:         _run("sec_edgar", sec_edgar_search)
450:     elif domain == "clinical":
451:         # I-meta-002-q1d (#942-clinical): add Europe PMC primary-literature breadth on top of generic
452:         # Serper + S2. Keyless/free + fail-open; kill-switch PG_CLINICAL_EUROPE_PMC=0. (ClinicalTrials.gov
453:         # + openFDA/DailyMed are named fast-follows — CT.gov runtime 403, openFDA needs an allowlist change.)
454:         if os.getenv("PG_CLINICAL_EUROPE_PMC", "1").strip() in ("1", "true", "True"):
455:             _run("europe_pmc", europe_pmc_search)
456: 
457:     return DomainBackendResult(
458:         domain=domain,
459:         candidates=candidates,
460:         backends_used=used,
461:         per_backend_counts=per,
462:     )

164: def test_r6_backend_exception_fails_open() -> None:
165:     """If a backend raises, the dispatcher swallows it and returns
166:     results from the other backends."""
167:     def _raising(q, limit):
168:         raise RuntimeError("API down")
169: 
170:     with patch(
171:         "src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends.arxiv_search",
172:         side_effect=_raising,
173:     ), patch(
174:         "src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends.github_search_repos",
175:         return_value=[SearchCandidate(
176:             url="https://github.com/x/y", title="repo",
177:             snippet="", source="github",
178:         )],
179:     ):
180:         result = run_domain_backends(
181:             domain="tech",
182:             research_question="anything",
183:         )
184:     # arxiv crashed but github still contributed
185:     assert len(result.candidates) == 1
186:     assert result.candidates[0].source == "github"
187: 
188: 
189: def test_r6_dispatcher_dedupes_across_backends() -> None:
190:     dup_url = "https://shared.example.com/page"
191:     with patch(
192:         "src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends.arxiv_search",
193:         return_value=[SearchCandidate(
194:             url=dup_url, title="via arxiv", snippet="", source="arxiv",
195:         )],
196:     ), patch(
197:         "src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends.github_search_repos",
198:         return_value=[SearchCandidate(
199:             url=dup_url, title="via github", snippet="", source="github",
200:         )],
201:     ):
202:         result = run_domain_backends(
203:             domain="tech", research_question="x",
204:         )
205:     assert len(result.candidates) == 1
206:     # First backend to surface wins (arxiv) — github drops as duplicate
207:     assert result.candidates[0].source == "arxiv"

210: # --- I-meta-002-q1d (#942-clinical): Europe PMC backend URL priority + skip + fail-open ----------
211: def _epmc_result(*, pmcid="", doi="", pmid="", title="t", abstract="a"):
212:     return {"pmcid": pmcid, "doi": doi, "pmid": pmid, "title": title, "abstractText": abstract}
213: 
214: 
215: def test_europe_pmc_url_priority_pmcid_first() -> None:
216:     # PMCID present (even with doi+pmid) → PMC full-text URL wins (Codex brief-gate: PMC is the
217:     # strongest keyless fetchable path).
218:     payload = {"resultList": {"result": [_epmc_result(pmcid="PMC555", doi="10.1/x", pmid="999")]}}
219:     with patch("src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends._http_get_json", return_value=payload):
220:         out = europe_pmc_search("metformin renal")
221:     assert out[0].url == "https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC555/"
222:     assert out[0].source == "europe_pmc"
223:     assert out[0].metadata["doi"] == "10.1/x"
224: 
225: 
226: def test_europe_pmc_url_priority_doi_then_pmid() -> None:
227:     payload = {"resultList": {"result": [
228:         _epmc_result(doi="10.1056/abc", pmid="111"),   # no pmcid → doi
229:         _epmc_result(pmid="222"),                       # only pmid → pubmed
230:     ]}}
231:     with patch("src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends._http_get_json", return_value=payload):
232:         out = europe_pmc_search("q")
233:     assert out[0].url == "https://doi.org/10.1056/abc"
234:     assert out[1].url == "https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/222/"
235: 
236: 
237: def test_europe_pmc_skips_record_with_no_resolvable_id() -> None:
238:     # A record with none of pmcid/doi/pmid is SKIPPED — never a europepmc.org landing URL.
239:     payload = {"resultList": {"result": [
240:         _epmc_result(title="no ids"),
241:         _epmc_result(doi="10.1/keep"),
242:     ]}}
243:     with patch("src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends._http_get_json", return_value=payload):
244:         out = europe_pmc_search("q")
245:     assert len(out) == 1
246:     assert out[0].url == "https://doi.org/10.1/keep"
247:     assert all("europepmc.org" not in c.url for c in out)
248: 
249: 
250: def test_europe_pmc_fails_open_on_empty_or_error() -> None:
251:     with patch("src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends._http_get_json", return_value=None):
252:         assert europe_pmc_search("q") == []
253:     with patch("src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends._http_get_json", return_value={"bad": "shape"}):
254:         assert europe_pmc_search("q") == []
255:     # Codex diff-gate iter-1 P1: a helper/network EXCEPTION must also fail open (not escape).
256:     with patch(
257:         "src.polaris_graph.retrieval.domain_backends._http_get_json",
258:         side_effect=RuntimeError("network down"),
259:     ):
260:         assert europe_pmc_search("q") == []

1: # GPU Serving + Amortization — Full-Power Cost-Per-Run (POLARIS verifier cluster)
2: 
3: Analyst: GPU serving + amortization cost analyst. Date: 2026-05-30.
4: DOMINANT cost line. Every figure labelled [MEASURED] or [PROJECTED] with source.
5: 
6: ## 1. GPU topology REQUIRED to serve all 3 verifier roles concurrently [MEASURED from config]
7: 
8: Source of truth: `config/serving/verifier_roles.yaml` + `docs/serving/verifier_serving_runbook.md:25-34` + `config/architecture/polaris_runtime_lock.yaml:59-88`.
9: 
10: | Role | Model | Precision | GPU | TP | Weights | Source |
11: |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
12: | Mirror | cohere/command-a-plus (218B/25B MoE) | bf16 full | **8× H100 80GB** | 8 | ~438 GB | verifier_roles.yaml:48-64 |
13: | Sentinel | granite-guardian-4.1-8b (dense 8B) | bf16 | **1× A100 80GB** | 1 | ~16 GB | verifier_roles.yaml:76-90 |
14: | Judge | qwen3.6-35b-a3b (MoE 3B active) | fp8 | **1× H100 80GB** | 1 | ~35 GB | verifier_roles.yaml:103-120 |
15: | Generator | deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro | — | **OpenRouter API (NOT GPU)** | — | — | runtime_lock.yaml:49-54 |
16: 
17: **Cluster = 9× H100 80GB + 1× A100 80GB.** Mirror's bf16 (~438 GB > 8×80=640 GB w/ KV) is what forces the 8×H100 box. Generator stays hosted (OpenRouter) per the lock; it is a token line, not a GPU line.
18: 
19: ## 2. Live per-GPU hourly prices (web-searched 2026-05-30, USD/GPU-hr)
20: 
21: | Provider | H100 80GB SXM | A100 80GB | Source URL |
22: |---|---|---|---|
23: | **Vast.ai** | **$2.28** median (floor $1.53) | **$1.07** | vast.ai/pricing/gpu/H100-SXM ; vast.ai/pricing/gpu/A100-SXM4 ; getdeploying/spheron $1.53–2.27 |
24: | **OVHcloud** (EU sovereign) | **$3.29** (H100-380, 1×H100) | ~$1.50 | sparecores.com/server/ovh/h100-380 ($3.2928/hr) ; getdeploying $2.99 |
25: | **Scaleway** (EU sovereign) | **~$2.95** (€2.73 SXM5) | ~$1.80 | scaleway.com/en/pricing/gpu ; getdeploying |
26: 
27: EUR→USD ~1.08. Vast H100/H200 inventory is **US-only** (runbook:53) — fine for the public-data DRB-EN benchmark, **disqualified for production clinical** (use OVH/Scaleway/Hetzner EU). Sovereignty rules forbid US *LLM-vendor* runtime calls, not EU GPU.
28: 
29: **Whole-cluster $/hr (9×H100 + 1×A100):**
30: - Vast: 9×$2.28 + 1×$1.07 = **$21.59/hr**
31: - OVH (EU): 9×$3.29 + 1×$1.50 = **$31.14/hr**
32: - Scaleway (EU): 9×$2.95 + 1×$1.80 = **$28.35/hr**
33: 
34: ## 3. Measured anchor + full-power wall-clock projection
35: 
36: [MEASURED] `outputs/honest_sweep_r3/.../manifest.json` + `run_log.txt:9,20,40`:
37: - cost $0.0641, 24 evidence rows, gen-cap 20, verifier OFF-GPU, deepener OFF, **single expansion (not 8 rounds)**.
38: - retrieval elapsed **193.0s** + generation **1455.1s** = **27.5 min wall**.
39: 
40: [PROJECTED] full-power scaling (config defaults are the shallow baseline; full-power are targets):
41: - gen feed 20→50 = **2.5×** (PG_LIVE_MAX_EV_TO_GEN, .env.example:82 / architecture.md:344) → gen wall 1455s → ~60.6 min
42: - retrieval 1 expansion → **8 rounds** → 193s → ~25.7 min
43: - 4-role GPU gate (NEW, was off): ~108–216 claims × 3 roles, Mirror two-pass; warm ≈ **6 min** (budget doc §1.1–1.3 anchor: 540 calls/role <2 min warm)
44: - **Total full-power wall = ~92 min sequential upper bound; ~70 min with retrieval/gen overlap.** = 1.17–1.54 hr.
45: 
46: ## 4. MODEL A — STEADY-STATE / WARM (server already up)
47: 
48: cost/run = cluster $/hr × wall-hr. Using realistic-overlap 70 min (1.167 hr) → sequential 92 min (1.54 hr):
49: 
50: | Provider | wall 70 min | wall 92 min | + Generator API ($0.40–$1.50/run) |
51: |---|---|---|---|
52: | **Vast** | $25.19 | $33.25 | **≈ $26 – $35 / run** |
53: | OVH (EU) | $36.32 | $47.95 | ≈ $37 – $49 / run |
54: | Scaleway (EU) | $33.08 | $43.66 | ≈ $34 – $45 / run |
55: 
56: ## 5. MODEL B — DEDICATED RENTAL amortized over golden-5
57: 
58: Cold-start: Mirror 438 GB download + 8-way TP bringup **1.6–2.6 hr** (budget doc §1.1); Judge/Sentinel minutes. Box-held hrs:
59: - LO = 1.6 cold + 5×1.17 + 0.5 teardown = **7.93 hr**
60: - HI = 2.6 cold + 5×1.54 + 0.5 teardown + 2.0 vLLM/identity-probe debug = **12.80 hr**
61: 
62: | Provider | batch total | ÷5 GPU/run | + Generator | **per run** |
63: |---|---|---|---|---|
64: | **Vast** | $171 – $276 | $34 – $55 | +$0.40–1.50 | **≈ $35 – $57 / run** |
65: | OVH (EU) | $247 – $399 | $49 – $80 | +gen | ≈ $50 – $81 / run |
66: | Scaleway (EU) | $225 – $363 | $45 – $73 | +gen | ≈ $45 – $74 / run |
67: 
68: Whole golden-5 proving batch (Vast, Model B): GPU $171–276 + 5×gen + storage ~$10 = **~$185–295**.
69: 
70: ## 6. Which model is honest for "cost per run"
71: 
72: - **Steady-state (Model A)** is honest *only if the cluster is genuinely already up and serving many runs* — it omits the 1.6–2.6 hr Mirror cold-start, which is real money on a per-batch basis.
73: - **For the golden-5 proving batch the HONEST number is MODEL B (dedicated, amortized): ≈ $35–$57/run on Vast.** Cold-start is paid once over 5 runs, so it cannot be hidden. Quote Model A ($26–$35) only for the marginal/6th-run-onward case on an already-warm server.
74: 
75: ## 7. Cross-check vs repo's own figures (reconciled)
76: 
77: - Repo `docs/vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md` §3: Scenario C economy **$250–320**, Scenario A full-bf16 **$750–920** — those are the **whole proving PROGRAM** (multi-day disciplined dev $525 + 3 bench sessions + storage), NOT per-run.
78: - My golden-5 batch ~$185–295 (Vast) sits below repo Scenario A because I model **no multi-day dev phase**, only the golden-5 session. The $525 dev-hold-hours gap (budget doc §0.3 — the dominant lever) is the entire difference.
79: - Live Vast H100 SXM $2.28 is ~39% cheaper than the repo's assumed $30/8×H100-box ($3.75/GPU-hr), so my totals sit at/below repo Scenario A — consistent.
80: - **Honesty flag:** repo budget treats benchmark compute as "<2 min warm" (§1.1). That holds for the *verifier calls alone*, but a full-power RUN is 70–92 min (8 retrieval rounds + 50-source generation), so the steady-state per-run GPU cost is **$25–48**, not the ~$10–15/run the repo's "<2 min" framing implies. The repo's session math undercounts wall-clock by ~5×; my Model A corrects it.

.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:13:All three are servable. The decisive correction vs. the original premise: **Command A+ is NOT the 111B dense Command A** — it is a **218B-total / 25B-active sparse MoE** (128 experts, 8 active + 1 shared). Sizing the Mirror as a 111B dense model would have under-provisioned it badly.
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:15:### 1.1 Mirror — Cohere Command A+
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:19:| **Self-host (vLLM)** | **2 × H100 (160 GB)** | w4a4 (NVFP4) | vLLM ≥ 0.21.0 | inline `<co>…</co: 0:[0]>` spans |
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:20:| Self-host bf16 | 8 × H100 / 4 × B200 | none | vLLM | inline `<co>` spans |
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:24:- bf16 weights ≈ 438 GB → **8 × H100 floor** (no quad-H100 bf16 option).
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:25:- **w4a4 = 2 × H100 (160 GB) minimum, NOT 1 × H100.** NVFP4 quantizes the **MoE experts only**; the attention path (Q/K/V/O, KV cache, attention compute) stays full precision. So footprint is **above** the naive 218B × 0.5 B = ~109 GB floor (working envelope ~110–150 GB) — a single 80 GB H100 fails.
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:26:- **A100 is NOT a viable w4a4 target.** NVFP4/FP8 are emulated on Ampere with zero memory/latency benefit; the official GPU table lists only Hopper (H100) and Blackwell (B200). Do not rent A100s expecting a 4-bit speedup.
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:32:**Recommendation — prefer Cohere-direct for the Mirror.** Cohere is Toronto-based; **Canada is sovereign-OK** per project posture. Cohere-direct (a) returns the JSON `citations` array directly (no `<co>`-span parsing), (b) **eliminates the single largest hardware burden in the stack** (2×H100/160 GB self-host), and (c) runs **full precision**, which matters because the Mirror exists *for its calibration* — see the w4a4-calibration risk in §6. Self-host w4a4 on 2×H100 is the fallback if managed-API sovereignty (Canada acceptable) or availability blocks the direct route. **Operator action:** confirm `cohere/command-a-plus` is exposed on Cohere's managed API (§5).
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:34:### 1.2 Sentinel — IBM Granite Guardian 4.1 8B
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:36:- **Hardware: trivial.** Dense decoder-only transformer (NOT Mamba-2 hybrid → no exotic engine). 8B bf16 ≈ 16 GB weights, ~24–32 GB practical → fits **1×A100 40/80 GB** comfortably; 4-bit (~5 GB GGUF) fits a 24 GB 4090. Apache-2.0.
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:44:### 1.3 Judge — Qwen 3.6-35B-A3B
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:47:- **Hardware (MoE — all 35B resident, ~3B active):** bf16 ≈ 70 GB → tight/OOM on one 80 GB H100 at 262K context (needs H200 141 GB or TP). **FP8 (`Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8`) ≈ 35 GB → fits one H100/H200 cleanly — recommended self-host target.** H100+ has FP8 tensor cores; **A100 lacks them → use INT8 on A100.** INT4 ≈ 21 GB fits a 24 GB GPU.
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:104:| **Mirror** | `cohere/command-a-plus` | slug OK | **Cohere-direct managed API (Toronto, Canada-OK)** — JSON `citations`, full precision; fallback = self-host w4a4 on 2×H100 via vLLM |
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:105:| **Sentinel** | `ibm-granite/granite-guardian-4.1-8b` | slug OK | self-host vLLM offline `generate()`, 1×A100 40/80GB or 4090@4-bit, `temperature=0.0`, `max_model_len=8192` |
.codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:106:| **Judge** | `qwen/qwen-3.6-35b-a3b` | **→ `qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b`** (remove extra hyphen) | **self-host FP8 (`Qwen/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-FP8`) on 1×H100/H200 via vLLM `guided_choice`** (hard enum). .codex\I-meta-002\feasibility_synthesis.md:109:- **Mirror → Cohere-direct (Canadian managed) primary.** Removes the heaviest hardware burden (2×H100), gives JSON citations natively, preserves full-precision calibration. Self-host w4a4 on 2×H100 is the documented fallback.

.codex\I-meta-002\codex_design_iter1_verdict.txt:50:- **Sentinel** (IBM Granite Guardian 4.1 8B): purpose-built RAG hallucination detector. RAGTruth BAcc 0.841. Catches Mirror's misses. Input: Generator's claims + Mirror's verdicts. Output: hallucination flag + span attribution.

Output: stable parseable terminal verdict per claim (`{verdict: VERIFIED|PARTIAL|UNSUPPORTED|FABRICATED|UNREACHABLE, confidence: 0-1, reason: str}`).

docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:14:I-meta-002 wires **three** new roles: Mirror, Sentinel, Judge. The Generator (DeepSeek V4 Pro, ~8×H200, the largest model in the stack, 1M context) is **already running on OpenRouter API** in the current benchmark code (`pathB_run_gate.py` pins `OPENROUTER_PROVIDER_ORDER`, single provider e.g. `deepinfra`; the 16 smoke runs on 2026-05-28 used the API path). There is **no supplied Vast price for a V4-Pro-capable box**, and folding an 8×H100 generator into a scenario would silently dominate every total. So:
docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:19:$30/hr buys the **whole** 8×H100 box; $7.47/hr buys the **whole** 2×H100 box; $4.13/hr is one H100. Every line below is `box-hours-held × box-rate`. The research's aggregate "≈355 GPU-hours" figure is a *per-GPU* unit and is **deliberately not reused here** — mixing it with a single $/hr would double-convert. We build bottom-up, per box, per the task's own (config / $/hr / dev-hours / benchmark-hours / subtotal) structure.
docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:22:The benchmark itself is cold-start-dominated and cheap (compute < 2 min). The real "how much do I pay Vast" answer is **how many hours you leave the boxes UP while debugging**. Same Mirror 8×H100 @ $30/hr:
docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:33:Command A+, Granite Guardian 4.1 8B, and Qwen 3.6-35B-A3B are all servable on OpenRouter/deepinfra (the gate's pinned provider). If dev + benchmark run through the API, **Vast spend is $0** and you pay only API tokens (≈ single-digit dollars — see §1.4). The self-host scenarios below are the **sovereign-self-host upper bound**: what it costs to prove the production cluster path on Vast public GPUs (acceptable because the benchmark uses PUBLIC DRB-EN data, no clinical data — see §5).
docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:44:### 1.1 Mirror — Cohere Command A+ (218B total / 25B active sparse MoE)

docs\vast_ai_budget_i_meta_002.md:155:> **⚠ QUALITY FLAG:** W4A4 calibration quality is **UNTESTED** for Command A+. Cheapest in dollars, but the quality risk lands on the exact role you cannot afford to degrade. **Not recommended for a quality-no-object posture.**

docs\process\polaris_task_cycle_proposal_2026_05_28.md:245:**Two money gates, not one** — because the 3 new roles have **no endpoint until a GPU is rented** (Vast balance `$0`; Cohere Command A+ and Granite Guardian 4.1 are not on OpenRouter; Generator/DeepSeek V4 Pro is the *only* one of four with a live endpoint today). You literally cannot connectivity-probe Mirror/Sentinel/Judge before rental. So:
docs\process\polaris_master_blueprint_2026_05_28.md:25:With that flag in hand, the claim goes to the Mirror, which is Cohere Command A Plus. The Mirror runs in two passes. One pass gives a grounded answer with citations. A separate pass, not in grounding mode, returns a clean calibration verdict. Two passes are needed because the citation mode and the structured-verdict mode cannot be combined on this model. Then the claim goes to the Sentinel, which is IBM Granite Guardian. The Sentinel answers a yes-or-no question about whether the claim is grounded. There is a lethal detail here: for this model, yes means ungrounded, a hallucination was detected, and no means grounded. Getting that polarity backwards would let fabrications walk straight through, so it is tested explicitly. Finally the claim reaches the Judge, which is Qwen. The Judge is the terminal decision-maker. It takes the Mirror's calibration, the Sentinel's flag, and the memory's cross-time flag, and it issues exactly one of five verdicts: verified, partial, unsupported, fabricated, or unreachable. The Judge is forced to pick one of those five and cannot wander off. After each claim, we append one line to a verdict ledger and bump a cursor. That is a checkpoint too.
docs\process\polaris_master_blueprint_2026_05_28.md:53:And here is the honest boundary, the line the operator must hear travel with any green verdict. Gate A proves the machine is correctly wired and that its control flow and contracts are right. It does not prove the models are good. A canned Sentinel "yes" proves the pipeline routes an ungrounded flag to the right place. It does not prove the real Granite Guardian would actually say "yes" when shown a real fabrication. Gate A validates wiring and logic. Only the first real rental, Gate B, validates model behavior. That boundary is not negotiable, and it is the honest answer to "is the machine ready to spend on."
docs\process\polaris_master_blueprint_2026_05_28.md:77:First, the cheap-real-fixture question, because the source docs genuinely disagree. The gap analysis and the feasibility doc say Cohere Command A Plus and Granite Guardian are not available on OpenRouter and must be self-hosted on a rented GPU. The budget doc says all three are servable on OpenRouter or deepinfra, which would put the cost floor near zero. This decides whether the dry-run fixtures are real cheap captures or hand-built from each model's contract. Codex should verify per-role OpenRouter availability before we rely on cheap real capture, and either way the fixtures must carry honest provenance labels, recorded-real versus hand-built.
docs\process\polaris_gap_analysis_2026_05_28.md:1:The advisor's three corrections are exactly right and supported by the active_issue.json I read (D4 branch-protection finding, D5 Qwen slug already approved, D6/D7 Vast.ai serving + $0 balance blocker). I'll fold them in. This is my final deliverable.
docs\process\polaris_gap_analysis_2026_05_28.md:20:**Gap one — the four-role pipeline.** The Generator role is fully wired and real: `src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py` reads the Generator model setting and builds a live client. The other three roles — Mirror, Sentinel, and Judge — are declared as settings in one file, `src/polaris_graph/llm/openrouter_client.py`, and referenced nowhere else. No code ever builds a client for them. There is no calibration pass, no hallucination-detector pass, no final-arbiter pass. The architecture lock at `config/architecture/polaris_runtime_lock.yaml` names all four roles correctly, and a freeze guard in `scripts/dr_benchmark/pathB_run_gate.py` deliberately blocks the gated smoke test until the lock is signed — so today neither the old path nor the new path actually runs four roles. The gap is large. The one main thing to do: build a role-dispatch layer that constructs a client for each of Mirror, Sentinel, and Judge and adds their three call sites after generation. Important wrinkle the operator already decided: two of these models — Cohere Command A+ (Mirror) and Granite Guardian (Sentinel) — are not available on OpenRouter, so they must be self-hosted on rented Vast.ai GPUs, while Generator and Judge stay on OpenRouter. That means the dispatch layer needs two serving modes, and the whole four-role base is blocked on one operator action: the Vast.ai account balance is currently zero and needs credit loaded before Mirror and Sentinel can run anywhere. Also note: the Judge model name in the lock still carries a small typo, but that fix is already approved and in flight under issue I-meta-002 — it is not a fresh discovery.

docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:87:V4 Pro is the **recall peak**, paying for it with the worst HHEM-2.3 of the candidate set. docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:93:1. **Highest open-weight recall** at AA-Omniscience Accuracy 43.3% — second is V4 Flash 37.2%. For a generator that must surface evidence across six domains for downstream verification, **recall is the load-bearing floor.** A generator that doesn't surface a fact cannot be corrected by the mirror, sentinel, or judge — they can only flag what was emitted, not invent what wasn't. This is the critical asymmetry that argues against picking Command A+ for the generator slot despite Command A+'s superior calibration.
docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:97:5. **Operator lock + reinterpretation.** Operator-lock "stop reverting to weaker models" applies; Command A+ has LOWER raw recall than V4 Pro, so even though it's newer, swapping V4 Pro→Command A+ is a recall regression for the generator role specifically.

docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:144:### Step B winner: **Cohere Command A+** — NEW pick, swaps in over Kimi K2.6
docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:150:3. **Native citation grounding architecture** — Command A+ has training-baked grounding-span citation generation. Cohere blog: "every generated claim can be tied to an explicit grounding span in the source documents, and this is trained into the model rather than being a post-processing step." This is **architecturally aligned with POLARIS's `[#ev:...]` provenance schema** — the mirror would emit citations in approximately the right shape natively, reducing the post-processing burden.
docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:152:5. **Hardware efficiency** — 218B sparse MoE with 25B active per token, runs on as few as 2 H100s (per Cohere blog and the W4A4 quantization release). Same order-of-magnitude infra as V4 Pro but on cleaner license.
docs\polaris_step_b_full_set_audit_2026_05_27.md:158:- **AA-Omniscience Accuracy 14.1% is low — and the implied abstention rate is high.** Reconciling the two published AA-Omniscience metrics: AA-Omniscience Non-Hallucination 86% + AA-Omniscience Accuracy 14.1% implies that ~86% of items are either non-answered-correctly OR abstained — and given Cohere's own framing ("the model knows its limits"), this is almost certainly dominated by abstention rather than non-answer-correct. **The mirror over-abstention risk flagged below in §Open questions may already be realized by the published metrics.** Operator must validate on POLARIS clinical corpus before lock — if Command A+ abstains on >30% of POLARIS claims, the mirror is not adding signal and the role swaps to **Kimi K2.6 runner-up.** Read the AA-Omniscience paper [arXiv 2511.13029](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.13029v1) for the exact non-answered-vs-abstained decomposition before locking.
